description.wishlist: A job you've saved and might apply to — nothing sent yet.
description.draft: You're preparing the application but haven't submitted it.
description.applied: Application submitted — waiting to hear back.
description.phone_screen: First call with a recruiter or hiring manager.
description.technical: Technical interview or take-home assignment stage.
description.final_round: Last interview stage before the company decides.
description.offer: The company made an offer — the decision is yours now.
description.accepted: You took their offer — the happy ending.
description.rejected: The company decided not to move forward.
description.ghosted: The company went silent and stopped responding.
description.withdrawn: You pulled out of the process yourself.
description.archived: Closed and hidden from day-to-day tracking.

errors.401: Your session is no longer valid. Sign in again to continue.
errors.403: You don't have access to that.
errors.409: This application was changed elsewhere. Reload and try again.
errors.422: Those details couldn't be saved. Check the form and try again.
errors.429: Too many requests. Wait a moment and try again.
errors.502: The server is unreachable right now. Try again shortly.
errors.refreshingStale: This application was changed elsewhere — refreshing to the latest version…
errors.urlRequired: Paste a job posting URL first.
errors.invalidCredentials: That email and password don't match an account.

code.unauthenticated: Your session is no longer valid. Sign in again to continue.
code.invalid_credentials: That email and password don't match an account.
code.invalid_passkey: Passkey sign-in failed. Try again, or sign in with your password.
code.forbidden: The demo account can't be deleted — it's shared, so everyone reviewing this project needs it.
code.stale_record: This application was changed elsewhere. Reload and try again.
code.invalid_transition: That status change isn't allowed from the current status.
code.validation_failed: Those details couldn't be saved. Check the form and try again.
code.passkey_verification_failed: That passkey couldn't be added. Try again.
code.invalid_url: That couldn't be read as a job posting URL.
code.prefill_blocked: That site blocks automated readers, so the posting couldn't be fetched from here.
code.rate_limited: Too many requests. Wait a moment and try again.
code.prefill_unreachable: That page couldn't be reached. Check it opens in your browser, then try again.
code.prefill_failed: No job posting could be read from that.
code.prefill_paste_too_long: That posting is too long to read in one go, even once its formatting is stripped. Trim it and try again.
code.push_unavailable: Push notifications aren't set up on the server right now.

field.status_inclusion: New applications can only start as wishlist, draft, or applied.
field.resume_too_long: The resume must be under 1 MB.
field.resume_not_a_pdf: The resume must be a PDF.
field.cover_letter_too_long: The cover letter must be under 1 MB.
field.cover_letter_not_a_pdf: The cover letter must be a PDF.
field.base_too_many_applications: Your account is full. Delete an application to add another one.
field.base_too_many_passkeys: Your account has reached its passkey limit. Remove one to add another.
field.base_too_many_push_subscriptions: Your account has reached its device limit for notifications. Disable them on another device to add this one.

home.headline: A job tracker built on a <accent>finite state machine</accent>.
home.lede: Thirteen states, an explicit transition table, and an immutable audit trail behind every change. Track an application from wishlist to offer without the pipeline ever lying to you about where it is.
home.pipelineNote: It is not a line. A job hunt goes quiet, or ends, and then starts again — so three closed states can return to Applied, and the audit trail keeps every step of the round trip.
home.pipelineSource: Illustration. The transition table lives in api/app/lib/application_fsm.rb
home.fsmBody: Thirteen states and a hand-written list of the moves between them. ghosted may return to applied; applied may not jump to offer. It is a plain Ruby object, not a state-machine gem.
home.auditBody: Every transition appends a timeline entry — who moved it, from which state, to which, and when. Nothing in the app updates one in place.
home.jobsBody: Follow-up reminders run on Solid Queue inside the Puma process. No Redis, no worker service. Each reminder is idempotent by key, so a retry cannot send it twice.
home.boardTitle: A board that reads the table
home.boardBody: Drag a card across the Kanban board and it runs a real transition. The board fetches the transition table from the API instead of keeping a copy, so only the columns a card may legally reach light up — and the server checks again anyway.
home.tagline: A job application tracker built on a finite state machine: thirteen states, an explicit transition table, and an immutable audit trail. Rails 8 API, Next.js 16 frontend, one PostgreSQL database.

about.title: Why it is built this way
about.lede: KarirKalyan tracks job applications. It is also where I settled four questions before writing any of it. Each one had an easier answer than the one I took, so here is what the easier answer would have cost.
about.railsTitle: Why Rails, when I write TypeScript
about.railsBody: My working life has been JavaScript. Tokyo runs on Rails, and you learn less about a framework's conventions by reading them than by shipping something inside them. So the API is Rails 8 — API-only, Devise and devise-jwt for auth — while the frontend stayed the Next.js I already knew. The unfamiliar half of the stack is the half I set out to learn, and it is the half with the tests.
about.fsmTitle: Why a plain object, not a state-machine gem
about.fsmBody: ApplicationFSM is a plain Ruby object wrapping a table of legal transitions. A gem would have given the same guarantees and put a DSL between me and them. Thirteen states fit in one readable array — including the revival edges, where ghosted, rejected and withdrawn each return to applied, because real job hunts do that. Transitions run through a service object rather than a model callback, so every status change in the system has a call site you can grep for.
about.jobsTitle: Why Solid Queue, not Sidekiq and Redis
about.jobsBody: One job needs to run once a day. Sidekiq would have meant a Redis instance and a worker process — two more things to deploy, monitor and pay for, in service of a single cron. Solid Queue keeps the queue in the Postgres database that already exists, and its workers run inside the Puma process. The deployed system is two services and one database, which is the whole argument.
about.filesBody: Resumes and cover letters are stored as bytea, capped at one megabyte, and served through dedicated download endpoints that keep them out of the ordinary JSON. Object storage is the right answer at scale and the wrong one here: a bucket, a set of credentials, and a signed-URL dance, for files that are small and few. Storing them in the row means the file and the record are backed up together — worth more, at this size, than a ceiling I will not reach.
about.closingBody: The demo account is seeded with twelve Tokyo tech applications spread across the pipeline. The source is public, and the API documents itself.

docs.title: The API, and how it behaves
docs.lede: The frontend you are reading is one client of a Rails 8 JSON API. Every endpoint is documented by its own request spec — the OpenAPI schema is generated from the tests, so it cannot drift from the implementation. This page is the shape of the thing; the Swagger UI is the reference.
docs.authBody: Signing in returns a JWT in the Authorization header — there is no sign-up endpoint, because registration is closed. In this frontend that token never reaches client-side JavaScript: a Next.js route handler receives it and writes an httpOnly cookie, and every later request is proxied server-side. Signing out rotates the token's jti, which revokes every device at once.
docs.scopeBody: Every record is reached through current_user.applications. Asking for someone else's application returns 404, not 403 — a 403 would confirm the row exists.
docs.errorsBody: Every failure responds with an error sentence and a stable machine-readable code; validation failures add a details array naming each field and its error type. This frontend localizes off the code, never the sentence. The status still groups them:

errorTable.409: An optimistic-locking conflict — the row changed under you.
errorTable.422: An illegal FSM transition, a validation failure, a bad pre-fill URL, or a site that refuses to be read by anything but a browser.
errorTable.502: A pre-fill that got nowhere: the page couldn't be fetched, or it was fetched and yielded no posting.
errorTable.503: The AI key is missing, or Postgres is down.

docs.paginationBody: The applications list takes ?after=…&limit=20 — limit clamped to 1–100, default 10 — and answers with a data array beside a meta object carrying next_cursor and has_more. The cursor is a Base64 timestamp. A malformed one is ignored and you get the first page rather than an error. Filters compose with it server-side.

endpoint.signOut: Revoke the token on every device.
endpoint.destroyAccount: Erase the account and everything under it. There is no sign-up endpoint to undo it with.
endpoint.index: List your applications, paginated and filterable.
endpoint.create: Create one. The status must be an entry state.
endpoint.prefill: Read a job posting URL and fill the fields with Claude.
endpoint.show: One application, with its valid next states and timeline.
endpoint.update: Edit the fields. Not the status — that is a transition.
endpoint.transition: Move the status through the state machine.
endpoint.resume: Download the stored resume as a PDF.
endpoint.coverLetter: Download the stored cover letter as a PDF.
endpoint.transitionTable: The state machine, read-only — every state and its legal next states.
endpoint.dashboard: Aggregate counts, the filter facets, ghost risk, and the current user.

docs.swaggerBody: Request and response schemas, every parameter, and a console to call the API live. Generated by rswag from the request specs.

privacy.title: What this app does with your data
privacy.lede: KarirKalyan stores resumes, which are close to the most personal document a person owns. This page says exactly what it holds, where it lives, and who else can see it. Every claim here is checkable against the source.
privacy.whoBody: KarirKalyan is run by Chairul Akmal, an individual — there is no company behind it. It is a portfolio project that is also used in earnest, not a business. Being an individual does not make the operator any less responsible for the data on this page, and this policy is written on that basis.
privacy.collectedBody: Your email address and password (stored only as a bcrypt hash, never in the clear), the job applications you enter, the status history the app writes as you move them through the pipeline, and — if you upload them — one resume and one cover letter per application. Nothing else is asked of you — there is no profile, no phone number, no address field: whatever is in the PDFs you upload is whatever the app holds about you. One thing is collected without being asked for, and it would be dishonest to leave it out: your IP address. The rate limiter counts requests against it, and if something breaks, the error report carries it. The request log does not — it records the time, a request ID and the parameters, but not who sent them. Your IP is not used for anything else, and it feeds no profile.
privacy.storedBody: One PostgreSQL database, managed by Railway. The PDFs are rows in that same database (bytea columns), not a separate file host. A backup runs daily — a pg_dump stored as an archive in a private repository, where it expires after 60 days. The database is not replicated anywhere else, and the operator keeps no working copy on a personal machine; restoring a backup to confirm it still restores is the exception, and that copy is deleted afterwards.
privacy.sharedBody: Five services, each doing one job: Railway hosts the app and the database; GitHub runs the nightly backup and stores it, which means an archive of the entire database — resumes included — sits in GitHub's storage for the 60 days before it expires; Resend delivers reminder digests to your address; Honeybadger receives error reports — only errors, never a record of the requests that worked — with request parameters filtered so passwords and email addresses do not appear in them; and Anthropic receives the text of the job posting you use the AI pre-fill on, and nothing else: never a resume, never a cover letter, never anything else from your account. That is the entire list. Nothing is sold, and nothing goes to an advertiser or a data broker. One thing worth saying because it works in your favour: the pre-fill is fetched by the server, not by your browser, so the site hosting the job posting sees a request from the server and never learns that you were the one reading it.
privacy.notCollectedBody: No analytics. No tracking pixels. No advertising. No third-party JavaScript — the content security policy blocks any that got injected. No cookie banner either, because there is nothing to consent to: two cookies, both strictly functional, neither one a tracker — the one that holds your session, and the one that remembers whether you chose English or Japanese.
privacy.exportBody: The dashboard has two export buttons and they are not decorative. The CSV is a spreadsheet of your applications; the account archive is a zip holding every field, every timeline entry, and every PDF you uploaded — enough to reconstruct the account somewhere else. Both are one click, no request, no waiting.
privacy.erasureBody: Email the address below and the operator will delete the account — expect a reply within a few days, since it is one person reading a mailbox rather than a queue. The deletion itself is total: the user row, every application, every timeline entry, and every uploaded PDF go with it in a single cascade, and the backup archives that still hold them expire within 60 days. There is deliberately no self-service delete button in the app. The endpoint exists (DELETE /api/v1/auth/account) and is what the operator runs; it is not wired to a button, because an irreversible action with no recovery path does not belong one mis-click away.
privacy.contactBody: Questions, data requests, or erasure requests: <mail></mail>. It is a mailbox a person reads.

terms.title: Terms of use
terms.lede: Short, because the service is small and honest about what it is.
terms.whatBody: KarirKalyan is a job application tracker, built and run by an individual as a portfolio project that is also used in earnest. It is free. It is not a company, not a product, and not sold to anyone.
terms.accountsBody: There is no sign-up. Accounts are created by the operator, and nobody can register one from the web. If you have an account, you are responsible for what happens under it — the password is the whole of the security.
terms.demoBody: The demo credentials are public — printed in the project's README, and shipped in the sign-in page's own JavaScript — so the demo account is shared and world-writable: anyone reading this can change or delete anything in it. Put nothing in it you would mind a stranger seeing, and expect it to be reset to its seeded state without warning.
terms.asIsBody: The service is provided as-is. There is no uptime commitment, no support obligation, and no guarantee that data survives — though the operator does back it up daily, and the account archive export exists precisely so that your copy does not depend on the operator's. The service can change or stop at any time.
terms.acceptableBody: Do not attack the service, script it, or use it to store anything unlawful. Rate limits are enforced in code, and an account that tries to work around them can be removed.
terms.changesBody: These terms may change. The date at the top says when they last did, and the full history is in the public git repository — every version of this page is a commit anyone can read.

auth.expired: Your session expired. Sign in again to continue.
auth.registrationClosed: Registration is closed — this app holds resumes, and it is deliberately not built to be anyone's custodian but its author's. The demo account above is the full app, with twelve applications already in it.

settings.title: Settings
settings.passkeysDescription: Sign in with your password manager, fingerprint, or face instead of typing a password. A passkey added here syncs through your password manager to your other devices — and your password keeps working either way.
settings.unsupported: This browser can't create passkeys. Password sign-in keeps working.
settings.createFailed: The passkey couldn't be created. Try again.
settings.confirmRemove: Remove this passkey? You can add it again later.
settings.pushDescription: Get the morning follow-up digest as a notification on this device, alongside the email. Nothing is asked until you press the button, and you can turn it off here any time.
settings.pushEnabled: This device receives the follow-up digest.
settings.pushDenied: Notifications are blocked for this site in your browser. To enable them, allow notifications in your browser's site settings, then come back here.
settings.pushUnsupported: This browser doesn't support push notifications. The email digest keeps working.
settings.pushFailed: Notifications couldn't be set up. Try again.

dashboard.title: Dashboard
dashboard.tracked: {count, plural, =0 {no applications tracked} one {# application tracked} other {# applications tracked}}
dashboard.avgDaysTooltip: Counts applications that reached offer, accepted, or declined. Measured from your applied date to when the offer status was recorded in the audit log.

exports.blurb: Take your history with you. The spreadsheet is a convenience view of your applications; the archive is everything — applications, timeline, and the resumes and cover letters you uploaded.

ghostRisk.summary: {count, plural, one {# application has} other {# applications have}} been quiet longer than a company usually takes to get back to you.
ghostRisk.silence: {days, plural, one {# day} other {# days}} of silence in {stage} — past your {threshold}-day mark
ghostRisk.markerTitle: Silent longer than a reply usually takes at this stage
ghostRisk.explain: Compares each waiting application against how long companies have actually taken to reply to you at that stage — the 90th percentile of your own response times. Until you have five replies at a stage, a general default is used instead.

basis.default: {stage}: {days} days — default, only {count} of the 5 replies needed

board.title: Kanban
board.tracked: {count, plural, =0 {no applications on the board} one {# application on the board} other {# applications on the board}}
board.truncated: Showing only the first {count} applications — the board is truncated. Use the dashboard filters to reach the rest.
board.staleTable: The server sent a transition table this version doesn't recognise — a deploy may be in progress. Try again in a moment.

list.allStagesHidden: Every stage is hidden, so there is nothing to list.
list.resultCount: {count, plural, =0 {No applications shown} one {# application shown} other {# applications shown}}

detail.finalState: {label} — {description} This is a final state, so the status can no longer change.

transitions.reopenable: Can be re-opened to Applied later.

files.tooLarge: That file is {size} MB — the limit is 1 MB.

newApplication.title: New application
newApplication.lede: Start in wishlist or draft. Status changes go through the FSM.
newApplication.prefillHint: Paste a Wantedly, LinkedIn, or company posting — Claude reads Japanese too. Review the fields before saving.
newApplication.prefillDone: Filled from the posting — review and edit before saving.
newApplication.pasteHint: That page can't be read from here, but you can. Open it in your browser, select the posting, and paste it below — Claude reads the text the same way either way.
newApplication.pasteCounter: {count, plural, =0 {Nothing pasted yet} one {# character pasted} other {# characters pasted}}
newApplication.statusHint: You can advance the status later from the application page.

delete.confirmPrompt: Delete permanently? This cannot be undone.

error.title: Something went wrong
error.body: The page failed to load — possibly a temporary network problem. Try again in a moment.

notFound.title: Page not found
notFound.body: That page does not exist or may have been removed.
